read.title: The Dispossessed
read.author: Ursula K. Le Guin
read.notes: Changed how I think about ownership and society. Le Guin uses sci-fi the way it should be used — to ask real questions.

read.title: Piranesi
read.author: Susanna Clarke
read.notes: Finished it in one sitting. The world-building is unlike anything I've read. Haunting and tender.

read.title: The Anthropocene Reviewed
read.author: John Green
read.notes: Unexpectedly moving. The essay on Auld Lang Syne wrecked me. Green is a better essayist than I expected.

read.title: Project Hail Mary
read.author: Andy Weir
read.notes: The most fun I've had reading in years. Rocky is one of the best characters in recent sci-fi. Pure joy.

read.title: Normal People
read.author: Sally Rooney
read.notes: The dialogue is extraordinary — so much conveyed in what isn't said. Connell and Marianne feel completely real.

read.title: Klara and the Sun
read.author: Kazuo Ishiguro
read.notes: Quietly devastating. Klara's perspective on humans is more insightful than most humans'. Ishiguro at his best.

read.title: The Midnight Library
read.author: Matt Haig
read.notes: Sweet and readable. The concept is better than the execution but it earns its ending.

read.title: Educated
read.author: Tara Westover
read.notes: Incredible story of self-invention. Couldn't put it down. What Westover survived is almost unbelievable.

read.title: The Left Hand of Darkness
read.author: Ursula K. Le Guin
read.notes: Even better than The Dispossessed in some ways. The exploration of gender and society is still radical decades later.

read.title: Tomorrow, and Tomorrow, and Tomorrow
read.author: Gabrielle Zevin
read.notes: Surprised me completely. A love story that refuses to be a love story. The game design sections are genuinely insightful.

read.title: How to Do Nothing
read.author: Jenny Odell
read.notes: Dense but worth it. Made me think about attention as a resource more carefully than anything else I've read.

read.title: The Three-Body Problem
read.author: Liu Cixin
read.notes: Ambitious and strange. The physics ideas are mind-bending. Sometimes the characters feel thin but the scale makes up for it.

read.title: Pachinko
read.author: Min Jin Lee
read.notes: Multigenerational and sweeping. Every character is fully realized. Stayed with me for months.

read.title: A Gentleman in Moscow
read.author: Amor Towles
read.notes: Delightful in every sense. Warmly written. The Count is one of the most charming protagonists I've encountered.

read.title: Crying in H Mart
read.author: Michelle Zauner
read.notes: Grief and food and cultural identity all braided together. Cried multiple times. Zauner's voice is extraordinary.

to_be_read.title: The Dark Forest
to_be_read.author: Liu Cixin
to_be_read.notes: Sequel to Three-Body Problem. People say it's even better.

to_be_read.title: Babel
to_be_read.author: R.F. Kuang
to_be_read.notes: Everyone I trust has loved this. Dark academia with teeth.

to_be_read.title: Bewilderment
to_be_read.author: Richard Powers
to_be_read.notes: Loved The Overstory. This is supposed to be similar but more intimate.

to_be_read.title: The Sympathizer
to_be_read.author: Viet Thanh Nguyen
to_be_read.notes: Won the Pulitzer. Multiple people have recommended this independently.

to_be_read.title: Demon Copperhead
to_be_read.author: Barbara Kingsolver
to_be_read.notes: Pulitzer winner. David Copperfield retelling set in the opioid crisis.

to_be_read.title: Never Let Me Go
to_be_read.author: Kazuo Ishiguro
to_be_read.notes: After Klara I want to read everything Ishiguro has written.

to_be_read.title: The Ministry for the Future
to_be_read.author: Kim Stanley Robinson
to_be_read.notes: Climate fiction. Dense and apparently more policy memo than novel in places but important.

to_be_read.title: Intermezzo
to_be_read.author: Sally Rooney
to_be_read.notes: New Rooney. After Normal People I'll read whatever she writes.
